lessons.title: Pack indexing
lessons.motivation:
Before C++26, getting a single specific element out of a pack was surprisingly awkward. The language gave you no direct way to say "give me the N-th element of this pack", so you had to take detours: recursing over the pack (peeling off one element per template instantiation), packing everything into a `std::tuple` just to use `std::get<N>`, or fold-expression tricks. Every one of these approaches is verbose, hurts compile times, and obscures the actual intent — "I just need element number N".

**Pack indexing** removes this hassle: it adds an ordinary subscript to packs, and accessing an element — by value or by type — becomes a one-liner that reads exactly like the thought behind it.
lessons.theory:
Pack indexing (proposal P2662) is a subscript syntax that picks a single element out of a pack by an index known at compile time. Previously, a pack could only be "expanded entirely" via `...`; now you're allowed to access it pointwise, almost like an array.

The syntax has two forms, distinguished only by context. When `pack...[N]` appears where an expression is expected, it's a **pack-index-expression**, and it yields the N-th value. When the same notation appears where a type is expected, it's a **pack-index-specifier**, and it yields the N-th type. The same `...[N]` carries two meanings depending on position, and the compiler figures out which one applies on its own.

The index is zero-based. The main requirement: `N` must be a **constant expression** — a value the compiler knows ahead of time — so you cannot index a pack with a runtime variable, and trying to do so is a compile error. That said, `N` can depend on template parameters: `sizeof...(Ts) - 1` is also a constant expression — that's how you grab the last element. If the index is out of range (for a pack of `k` elements, valid indices are `0` through `k-1`), the program becomes **ill-formed**: again diagnosed at compile time, with no undefined behavior. This is a deliberate C++26 line — what used to be silent UB in the old language is caught by the compiler here (more on this in Module 2).

You can index any pack: a function parameter pack, a template parameter pack (both type and non-type), a pack from a generic lambda, and also a structured-binding pack (the latter via P1061, a separate lesson). Value category matters too: when indexing a function parameter pack, the expression `args...[i]` names a specific parameter — that is, it's an lvalue — so you can take a reference to it or its address.

Finally, the limits: pack indexing gives you neither slicing nor runtime-value selection — a direct consequence of the constant-expression requirement. If you need runtime selection, you still build an array or tuple and index that.

`pack...[N]` follows the same `decltype` rules as an ordinary variable name: `decltype(xs...[0])` gives the parameter's declared type without a reference — just like `decltype(x)` for a variable `x` — while `decltype((xs...[0]))`, with extra parentheses, gives a type that reflects value category, i.e. `T&` for an lvalue. It's the same long-familiar "id-expression vs. parenthesized expression" pair of rules, just applied to a pack.

Being ill-formed due to an out-of-range index is a special case of a template-argument substitution error, and SFINAE applies to it: if `pack...[N]` with an invalid `N` appears in the immediate context of a substitution (for example, in a trailing return type via `decltype`), the compiler doesn't fail the whole build — it simply drops that overload or specialization from the candidate set, like any other type error under SFINAE. That turns `...[N]` into a ready-made "does the pack have at least N+1 elements" check, without `requires` or `enable_if`.

That said, pack indexing doesn't replace `std::tuple` and `std::get<N>`: a pack is a template abstraction over the parameters of the current instantiation, not an object with its own storage and lifetime. If a set of values needs to be stored, copied, or passed around as a single entity, `std::tuple` remains the right choice; `...[N]` is about reaching into a pack's parameters in place, without wrapping them.

examples.title: Picking the N-th value
examples.explanation: The notation `args...[1]` directly retrieves the pack's second value — no recursion, no intermediate tuple.

examples.title: Last element + both forms
examples.explanation: The index `sizeof...(Ts) - 1` is itself a constant expression, so it's legal. The same `...[N]` serves both the value pack `xs` and the type pack `Ts`; the fact that the `static_assert` compiles proves that `last_t<int, double, char>` is `char`.

examples.title: The type form right in the signature
examples.explanation: Here `Ts...[0]` sits exactly where a return type is normally written — that's the essence of pack-index-specifier: it fits any position where a type is expected. The pack's first type is `double`, so the function returns `double`.

examples.title: A pack element is an lvalue
examples.explanation: `xs...[0]` isn't a copy — it's the name of the first parameter, so you can bind `auto&` to it and modify its value. `10` becomes `15`, and the second access returns the already-modified value.

examples.title: SFINAE: index as a check on pack size
examples.explanation: For the pack `{10, 20}` (size 2, valid indices 0..1), `xs...[2]` is out of range — an error in the first overload's trailing return type. But that error occurs in the immediate context of substitution, so SFINAE kicks in: the first overload is quietly removed, the second remains, and the call returns `-1`. For a pack of three or more elements, the first overload would have been chosen.

exercises.explanation: Index 2, zero-based → the third element of `5, 6, 7, 8`, which is `7`. A non-type template parameter pack is indexed the same way as a value pack.

A pack index must be a constant expression, but `i` is a runtime parameter. So `v...[i]` won't compile: pack indexing has no runtime form. For runtime selection, you put the elements into an array/tuple instead.

exercises.prompt: `f(1, 2, 3)` is instantiated, where `f` is defined as `constexpr auto f(T... v) { return v...[3]; }`. What happens?
options: Returns the last element, 3
Returns 0
Compile error: index out of range (ill-formed)
Undefined behavior at runtime
exercises.explanation: For a pack of three elements, valid indices are 0..2, so `[3]` is out of range → ill-formed, caught at compile time. No UB — this is C++26's safety line (Module 2).

exercises.prompt:
Given two overloads:
```cpp
template <typename... Ts>
auto third(Ts... xs) -> decltype(xs...[2]) { return xs...[2]; }

template <typename... Ts>
int third(Ts...) { return -1; }
```
What does `third(10, 20)` return?
options: 30 — index 2 is taken from the padded pack
Compile error: index out of range — the program is ill-formed
-1 — the first overload is dropped by SFINAE, leaving the second
Undefined behavior at runtime
exercises.explanation: For the pack `{10, 20}`, index 2 is out of range, so `decltype(xs...[2])` in the first overload's trailing return type is a substitution error in the immediate context. SFINAE removes that overload from the candidate set, leaving the second — it's called and returns `-1`.

questions.prompt: What must `N` be in `pack...[N]`?
options: a runtime value
a constant expression
a pointer
any int

questions.prompt: For a pack of size 4, the valid indices are:
Indexing a pack out of range is:
options: UB at runtime
a default value
an infinite loop
ill-formed at compile time

questions.prompt: For a function parameter pack `xs` whose first parameter has type `int`, what does `decltype((xs...[0]))` (with extra parentheses) give?
options: int
int&
int&&
const int

lessons.title: Placeholder `_`
lessons.motivation:
Before C++26, the "I don't care what this is called" variable had no good answer. You could already ignore a structured-binding element (`auto [x, _, z] = f();`) — `_` was just an ordinary legal name — but reusing it in the same scope was a redefinition error, so every "don't need this" name had to be unique (`unused1`, `tmp2`, ...) and manually marked `[[maybe_unused]]`, or the compiler would complain about an unused variable.

This was especially painful for RAII guards that exist purely for their destructor's side effect (`std::lock_guard`, `std::scoped_lock`, etc.): every such guard in a function needed its own name, and a forgotten name turned `std::lock_guard<std::mutex>(mtx);` into a temporary that's destroyed immediately — without holding the lock at all.

**Placeholder `_`** (proposal P2169) makes `_` a special name: it can be declared any number of times in the same scope without a redefinition error, and such declarations implicitly get `[[maybe_unused]]` — the compiler stops complaining about an "unused" variable that was never meant to be used.
lessons.theory:
`_` (proposal P2169, "A nice placeholder, with no name") introduces the notion of a **name-independent declaration** — a declaration whose only declarator-id is `_`. This covers: local variables with automatic storage duration, non-static data members, structured-binding names, and lambda init-captures. Two special rules apply to them.

First: redeclaring `_` in the same scope is not a redefinition. Each `_` introduces a distinct entity with its own type, initializer, and lifetime; the compiler simply skips the usual "name already taken" check for it. Second: such a declaration implicitly gets `[[maybe_unused]]` — neither `-Wunused-variable` nor `-Wunused-but-set-variable` fires, even if `_` is never used after being declared.

These rules don't apply to every `_`. Declarations that have linkage or an identity beyond the immediate scope are *not* name-independent declarations: namespace-scope (including global) variables, `static`/`thread_local` local variables, function and lambda parameters, and names of functions and types. For these, `_` remains an ordinary name: a repeated `int _;` at namespace scope, or `static int _; static double _;` inside a function, is a plain redefinition error (GCC spells this out explicitly: "variable/static variable is not name-independent"), and a lone `_` parameter still triggers `-Wunused-parameter`.

Name lookup for `_` works like this: if exactly one name-independent `_` is declared in a scope, it can be referred to like any ordinary variable — it's a legal, if unremarkable, identifier, just as before C++26. But as soon as a second (or later) `_` appears in the same scope, any attempt to name `_` becomes **ambiguous** — the program is ill-formed at compile time, with no UB. In other words: from the second `_` onward, the name becomes "write-only"; referring to it is caught by the compiler as a diagnostic, not turned into undefined or surprising behavior.

The same applies to structured bindings: `auto [x, _, _] = f();` — both `_`s are valid and don't conflict with each other, whereas `auto [x, y, y] = f();` was, and remains, ill-formed because of the repeated "real" name `y`.

The idiom particularly pays off for RAII guards used purely for their destructor's side effect: `std::lock_guard<std::mutex> _{mtx};` is a fully-fledged named variable with a normal lifetime to the end of its scope (the mutex stays held for the whole block), and its "unnamability" afterward is exactly what's wanted — nothing else is ever done with the guard. Several such guards can be declared one after another in the same function, each named `_` — that's the main payoff of "redeclaration isn't a redefinition".

The remaining checklist items don't apply directly: `_` is a question of the name in a declaration, not of an expression, so value category, SFINAE, and behavior in unevaluated contexts (`decltype`, `sizeof`, etc.) get no new rules — there, `_` follows the same name-lookup ambiguity rule as in evaluated code (if `_` is declared twice, `decltype(_)` is just as ill-formed as `_` alone). In templates, name-independence is a property of the declaration itself, not of the instantiation result, so it behaves the same in ordinary and templated code. An explicit `[[maybe_unused]]` on `_` is redundant but harmless — the attribute just duplicates what's already implied.

examples.title: Ignoring a structured-binding element and holding an RAII guard
examples.explanation: `_` in the second slot of the structured binding simply discards the middle element of the triple — no extra name, no `[[maybe_unused]]`. Likewise, `std::lock_guard<std::mutex> _{mtx}` is an ordinary local variable: it lives (and holds the mutex) to the end of the block, just like any named guard, but it can't be referred to by name afterward — which is exactly the point.

examples.title: Multiple `_`s in one scope: two RAII guards
examples.explanation: Two `std::lock_guard<std::mutex> _{...}` declarations in the same function are name-independent declarations: each introduces its own entity with its own lifetime, both live to the end of `critical()`, and both mutexes stay locked. Before C++26 you'd have had to invent `guard1`/`guard2` (or leave one anonymous -- with the immediate-destruction risk that comes with that).

exercises.explanation: `minmax_sum(5, 2, 9)` returns `{min, sum, max} = {2, 16, 9}`. The middle element (the sum) is discarded via `_`, so only `lo = 2` and `hi = 9` are printed.

Both `int _ = ...;` declarations are fine on their own (name-independent declarations don't cause a redefinition). But once there are two, the name `_` becomes ambiguous -- line 6, `std::print("{}\n", _)`, fails to compile: "reference to `_` is ambiguous", with two equally valid candidates.

questions.prompt: `int _ = ...;` is declared twice as local variables in the same scope. What happens?
options: A redefinition error, like for any other name
The second declaration introduces a separate entity -- not a redefinition
The second declaration shadows the first
Error: `_` can only be declared once per program

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a name-independent declaration (i.e. `_` behaves there like an ordinary name)?
options: a local variable `_` with automatic storage duration
a structured-binding name `_`
a variable `_` at namespace scope
a lambda init-capture `_`

questions.prompt: Two name-independent `_`s are declared in a scope. What happens when you try to refer to `_` by name?
options: It refers to the latest declaration (like shadowing)
UB at runtime
Compile error: the reference to `_` is ambiguous
It refers to the first declaration

questions.prompt: What's the problem with `std::lock_guard<std::mutex>(mtx);` (no variable name) compared to `std::lock_guard<std::mutex> _{mtx};`?
options: No difference -- both hold the lock to the end of the block
The first creates a temporary that's destroyed immediately -- the mutex isn't held
The second doesn't compile before C++26
The first is a compile error

lessons.title: = delete("reason")
lessons.motivation:
`= delete` has been around since C++11 and remains the standard way to say "this function exists in the overload set, but you may not call it" — banning copies, ruling out unwanted overloads, marking a template specialization unavailable. The catch is that `= delete` on its own explains nothing: the compile error just says "function is deleted", and the *why* has to be looked up in a comment near the declaration (if there even is one) or in documentation.

Before C++26, the only way to get the reason in front of whoever sees the error was a comment next to `= delete` — which the compiler obviously never shows in its diagnostic. Or you'd resort to a trick: instead of `= delete`, write `static_assert(false, "reason")` inside a template body, just to get a meaningful message in a templated context — but that doesn't work for ordinary (non-template) functions, and it muddles the very idea of "this function is unavailable by design".

**`= delete("reason")`** (proposal P2573) lets you attach a string literal to `= delete` that the compiler folds straight into the diagnostic text when something tries to call the deleted function — the reason becomes part of the compile error itself, not a comment nobody reads at the moment it matters most.
lessons.theory:
`= delete("reason")` is a syntactic extension of the existing `= delete`: after the `delete` keyword, parentheses hold a string literal explaining why the function is deleted. The deletion rule itself doesn't change in the slightest — only what the programmer sees in the error message changes.

**Where it applies.** A reason can be attached anywhere a plain `= delete` could already appear: ordinary functions and their overloads (`void f(int) = delete("...")`), special member functions — the copy constructor, copy-assignment operator, move constructor, destructor (`Foo(const Foo&) = delete("no copying")`), overloaded operators including `operator new`/`operator delete`, and function templates as a whole or individual explicit specializations (`template <typename T> void h(T) = delete("...")`). `= delete` doesn't gain any new positions — only the existing positions gain a richer spelling.

**Format of the reason.** The reason is an **ordinary string literal** (`"..."`), not an arbitrary constant expression. Adjacent string literals can be joined via plain C++ adjacent-literal concatenation (`"use " "f(long) instead"` becomes the single string "use f(long) instead" already at the lexing stage — a general language rule, not something special to `= delete`). But you cannot pass a variable, a `constexpr` `const char*`, or a computed expression: `delete(some_constexpr_string)` is ill-formed at compile time — GCC requires an actual string-literal token at this syntactic position (a diagnostic along the lines of "expected string-literal"). This is a deliberate restriction: the reason is part of the compiler's diagnostic text, not a program value, so there's no point — and no mechanism — for computing it at compile time.

**What the compiler shows.** When something tries to call (or, more generally, overload resolution selects) a function deleted with a reason, the compiler folds the reason text straight into the main error message — for example, `error: use of deleted function 'void f(int)': use f(long) instead`. Without a reason, it would just be `error: use of deleted function 'void f(int)'`. Nothing else about the error changes: it's still "use of deleted function" — the same error category as plain `= delete` — `= delete("reason")` doesn't change *when* a function is considered unavailable, only *how that's reported*.

**Effect on overload resolution and SFINAE — none.** This is the important point: adding a reason **does not change** overload resolution rules in any way. A deleted function — with or without a reason — still participates in overload resolution on equal footing with other candidates; the error only arises if *that* candidate ends up chosen as the best match. If the overload set has a deleted-with-reason `f(int)` and an ordinary working `f(long long)`, and the call `f(42LL)` unambiguously resolves to `f(long long)`, the program compiles without a single mention of the deleted overload or its reason — exactly as it would with a plain reasonless `= delete`. In other words, `= delete("reason")` is an improvement to *error-time diagnostics*, not a new mechanism for conditionally excluding overloads (that's still the job of `requires`/SFINAE/`enable_if`, entirely unrelated to `= delete`).

**Ill-formed vs UB vs diagnostic.** Calling a function deleted with a reason is a **compile error** (ill-formed), of the same nature as calling a function deleted without one: the program simply fails to compile — there's no UB and never was a "runs, but incorrectly" path here. C++26 doesn't change the error category here — it just makes the error message more informative.

**Unevaluated contexts, value category, decltype.** These checklist items don't apply directly: `= delete("reason")` doesn't introduce a new expression or produce a value — it's part of the function's *declaration*, not an expression. A deleted function (with or without a reason) participates in `decltype`, `noexcept`, `sizeof`, and other unevaluated contexts exactly the same before and after C++26: if such a context doesn't actually *call* the deleted function (it just inspects its type/signature), no call happens and no deleted-function error arises at all — nothing has changed here relative to C++11's `= delete`.

**Old alternative.** Before C++26, the only ways to communicate a reason were a comment next to `= delete` (which the compiler never shows in diagnostics) or, for templates, replacing `= delete` with a body containing `static_assert(false, "reason")`, which produces a static_assert-style message but requires the template to be instantiated and doesn't work for non-template overloads or special member functions. `= delete("reason")` doesn't make these obsolete (a comment is still useful for someone reading the header without calling the function), but for *calling* code it gives a more direct and uniform mechanism — one that works for every form of `= delete`, not just templates.

examples.title: A deleted overload doesn't get in the way of the working one
examples.explanation: `process(int)` is deleted with a reason, but the call `process(42LL)` resolves to `process(long long)` -- the working, non-deleted overload. The program compiles and runs normally: the deleted-with-reason overload participates in overload resolution on equal footing, but isn't selected, and its reason never makes it into a diagnostic. If `42LL` were plain `42` (type `int`) instead, the call would resolve to `process(int)`, and compilation would fail with `use of deleted function 'void process(int)': use process(long long) instead -- avoid silent truncation`.

examples.title: Deleted special member functions with a reason
examples.explanation: The copy constructor and copy-assignment operator are deleted with a reason explaining why: `Resource` owns a unique handle, and implicit copying is banned by design, not because someone forgot to define it. Meanwhile, move (`= default`) and the explicit `clone()` remain available and work as usual. If someone wrote `Resource b = a;` (a copy without `clone()`), compilation would fail with `use of deleted function 'Resource::Resource(const Resource&)': Resource owns a unique handle; use clone() to copy explicitly` -- the reason immediately points at what to do instead of copying.

exercises.explanation: `emit(int)` is deleted with a reason, but the argument to `emit(1.5)` is `double`, so overload resolution picks `emit(double)`, not the deleted overload. A deleted-with-reason function participates in overload resolution as usual -- it just doesn't win it -- so the program compiles and prints `1.5 s`.

`l.log("startup")` passes a `const char*`, so overload resolution selects the deleted overload `log(const char*)`. Compilation fails at line 15 with `use of deleted function 'void Logger::log(const char*)': Logger::log(const char*) is unsafe; use log(std::string_view) instead`. There's nothing else wrong with the code -- `log(int)` is defined and would work fine if it were the one selected.

questions.prompt: What does `= delete("reason")` add compared to plain `= delete`?
options: A new way to exclude overloads from overload resolution
The reason text appears in the compiler's diagnostic when a call to the deleted function is selected
The ability to call the "deleted" function at runtime with a warning
constexpr-evaluation of the reason at compile time

questions.prompt: What can `"reason"` be in `= delete("reason")`?
options: Any constant expression of type `const char*`
Only an ordinary string literal (adjacent literals may be joined by string-literal concatenation)
The name of a variable declared `constexpr`
A call to a `constexpr` function returning a string

questions.prompt: An overload set has `f(int)`, deleted with a reason, and a working `f(long long)`. The call `f(42LL)` resolves to `f(long long)`. What happens at compile time?
options: A compile error showing the reason from `f(int)`, because the deleted overload is also considered
The program compiles cleanly: the deleted overload participates in resolution but isn't chosen, and its reason never appears in any diagnostic
A warning, but not an error
UB at runtime, since both overloads are visible to the compiler

questions.prompt: Which declarations can `= delete("reason")` be applied to?
options: Only ordinary (non-template, non-member) functions
Only special member functions (constructors, assignment operators)
The same positions where plain `= delete` was already legal: ordinary functions/overloads, special member functions, operators, function templates and their specializations
Only function templates, as a replacement for `static_assert(false, ...)`

lessons.title: Structured bindings as pack (P1061)
lessons.motivation:
Structured bindings (C++17) can decompose a `std::tuple`, `std::pair`, aggregate, or array into named parts — but only if you know in advance how many parts there will be and spell out a name for each one. `auto [a, b, c] = f();` only works when `f()` returns exactly three elements; for generic code, where the number of elements depends on template parameters, structured bindings were useless — you were stuck with element-by-element `std::get<N>` or recursive helpers along the lines of "peel off the first element, recurse on the rest".

**Structured bindings as a pack** (proposal P1061) lifts this restriction: one of the elements in a binding can be declared as `...name` — it scoops up all the elements not claimed by the other names, forming a **pack** (`name` is a pack, in the same sense as a template parameter pack). Now `auto [first, ...rest] = some_tuple;` works for a tuple of any size ≥ 1: `first` gets the first element, and `rest` becomes a pack of everything that's left. This pack is an ordinary pack in the sense of `m1-l1`: `sizeof...`, `...[N]` (pack indexing), and `...` expansion in calls and initializers all apply to it.
lessons.theory:
P1061 extends the structured-binding-list with one new kind of element: instead of an ordinary name `name`, you can write `...name` — a **structured-binding pack**. **At most one** such element is allowed in a binding list, but its position is free: it can sit at the start, the end, or in the middle. `auto [first, ...rest] = t;`, `auto [...init, last] = t;`, and `auto [head, ...mid, tail] = t;` are all valid (confirmed against the compiler). The pack claims exactly as many elements as needed so that the total number of bindings matches the number of elements in the source: for a source `t` with `k` elements and `m` ordinary names in the list, the pack gets `k - m` elements. If `k - m` is zero, the pack is **empty** (`sizeof...(rest) == 0`) — that's well-formed, not an error. If `k < m` (there are more ordinary names than elements in the source, so the pack can't even reach zero), the program is **ill-formed** at compile time — exactly the same "N names provided for structured binding ... decomposes into K elements" diagnostic that pre-C++26 structured bindings already gave for a mismatched count of names: a pack doesn't introduce a new error category, it just means `m` can now be less than `k`, with the difference `k - m` flowing into the pack.

**What can be decomposed.** The sources are the same as for structured bindings in general, and they behave the same with a pack: `std::tuple`/`std::pair` (via the tuple protocol — `std::tuple_size`/`std::tuple_element`/`get<N>`), raw arrays (`T arr[N]`), `std::array`, and any user type implementing the tuple protocol (specializations of `std::tuple_size`/`std::tuple_element` plus a `get<I>` found via ADL or as a member). For arrays and `std::array`, a pack from a structured binding behaves the same as a pack from the tuple protocol — `auto& [head, ...tail] = arr;` gives `tail` as a pack of references to the remaining array elements.

**Reference qualifiers — old rule, extended uniformly to the pack.** Even before P1061, structured bindings already determined whether each bound element is an lvalue reference, a const reference, or an independent copy — depending on what's written before `[`: `auto`, `auto&`, `const auto&`, or `auto&&`. P1061 introduces no new rules here: every element of the pack gets exactly the same reference-ness that an ordinary bound element in the same position would get under the same qualifier. `auto [a, ...rest] = t;` — `rest` is a pack of independent copies; `auto& [a, ...rest] = t;` — `rest` is a pack of lvalue references into `t`, and `rest...[0] = 42;` mutates `t` itself; `const auto& [a, ...rest] = t;` — a pack of `const` references. And, as for any other named entity, the *name* of a pack element is an lvalue regardless of whether it's a reference or a copy: `decltype(rest...[0])` (no parentheses) gives the declared type without a reference (just like for an ordinary variable), while `decltype((rest...[0]))` (with parentheses) gives `T&` for copies and lvalue references, and `const T&` for const references — the same id-expression/parenthesized-expression pair of rules from `m1-l1`, applied to a structured-binding pack.

**Connection to pack indexing (`m1-l1`).** A pack obtained from a structured binding is a pack in the same sense as a function parameter pack or a template parameter pack: every operation from `m1-l1` applies to it. `rest...[0]` retrieves a specific element by a constant-expression index (with the same range rules — out of bounds is ill-formed at compile time, no UB), `sizeof...(rest)` gives the pack's current size, and `rest...` expands it in full — for example into a `std::tuple{rest...}` constructor or as call arguments. Unevaluated contexts (`decltype`, `sizeof`, `noexcept`) and SFINAE for a structured-binding pack follow exactly the same rules as for any other pack — the feature introduces no new cases.

**A practical limitation of current compilers.** As of this writing, a structured binding with a pack must appear in a dependent context — that is, inside a function template, a class-template method, or a generic lambda (which itself compiles as a template). `auto [a, ...rest] = t;` written directly in `main()`, with no enclosing template, is rejected by GCC trunk with `structured binding pack outside of template`. The standard doesn't formally require this — a zero-element pack in non-template code would simply mean a trivial structured binding — but in practice, today, such code belongs inside a template function or generic lambda.

**Old alternative.** Before C++26, "take the first element plus a pack of the rest" in generic code meant `std::get<0>(t)` for the first element and a recursive helper using `std::index_sequence`, which assembles the "tail" into a new `std::tuple` via `std::get<Is + 1>(t)...` — noticeably more verbose, requires a separate helper function with its own index pack, and gives no single readable "first + rest" expression. A structured-binding pack collapses all of that into one declaration line.

**Nuance checklist.** Unevaluated contexts and SFINAE — see above, inherited unchanged from `m1-l1`. Value category — determined by the reference qualifier before `[`, same as for structured bindings in general (see above). Old alternative — `std::get<N>` plus `index_sequence` recursion, see above. Ill-formed vs UB — too few elements (`k < m`) and an out-of-range pack-indexing index are both ill-formed at compile time, no UB; an empty pack (`k == m`) is well-formed. Interaction with the familiar — structured bindings and pack indexing, both already covered in the theory above.

examples.title: First element + a pack of the rest
examples.explanation: `first` takes the tuple's first element, and `...rest` becomes a pack of the remaining three. `sizeof...(rest)` and `rest...[0]` (pack indexing from `m1-l1`) work on it like on any other pack.

examples.title: A pack from an array, by reference
examples.explanation: `auto& [head, ...tail] = arr;` binds `head` and the pack `tail` as lvalue references to the array's elements — the same reference rule as for structured bindings without a pack, just extended to `tail`. Mutating `head` mutates `values[0]`.

examples.title: A pack that isn't last in the list
examples.explanation: A binding list may contain one pack in any position — not only last. Here `...front` claims the first three elements, and `last` is the fourth. P1061 also allows `[a, ...mid, z]` (pack in the middle) and `[...all]` (pack covering everything).

examples.title: Expanding the pack into a new tuple
examples.explanation: `rest...` expands the three-element pack into the `std::tuple{...}` initializer, producing a new `std::tuple<int,int,int>` — exactly as any other pack would expand.

exercises.explanation: A tuple of 5 elements: `a` takes the first (`5`), and `rest` becomes a pack of the remaining four (`6, 7, 8, 9`). `sizeof...(rest) == 4`, and `rest...[1]` is the second element of the pack (zero-based), i.e. `7`.

The list has three ordinary names (`a, b, c`) plus the pack `rest`, but `std::tuple{1, 2}` only has 2 elements. Even an empty pack (`sizeof...(rest) == 0`) doesn't save it: there are more ordinary names (3) than elements in the source (2), so the binding is ill-formed at compile time — line 6, `auto [a, b, c, ...rest] = t;` ("4 names provided for structured binding ... decomposes into 2 elements").

exercises.prompt: Given `auto [...mid, z] = std::tuple{1, 2, 3, 4};` — the pack comes first, before the single ordinary name `z`. What happens?
options: Compile error: the pack must be the last element of the binding list
Well-formed: `z = 4`, and `mid` is a pack of `1, 2, 3` (`sizeof...(mid) == 3`)
Well-formed, but `mid` is always empty and `z` gets the whole tuple
Compile error: only one name is allowed in a binding list when a pack is present
exercises.explanation: P1061 allows at most one pack in a binding list, but doesn't constrain its position: the pack can be first, last, or in the middle. Here `z` takes the last element (`4`), and `...mid` takes everything before it (`1, 2, 3`), forming a pack of size 3.

`auto&` before the binding list makes `a` and the whole pack `rest` lvalue references into `t` — same as for structured bindings without a pack. `rest...[0]` names `std::get<1>(t)`, so `rest...[0] *= 10` changes `t`'s second element from `2` to `20`; `t` after the call is `{1, 20, 3}`.

questions.prompt: How many structured-binding-pack elements can appear in one binding list `auto [...] = ...;`?
options: Any number, but all must be consecutive
At most one, in any position in the list (first, last, or in the middle)
At most one, and only in the last position
Exactly one per ordinary element

questions.prompt: `auto [a, b, ...rest] = std::tuple{1, 2};` — what happens?
options: Well-formed: `rest` is an empty pack, `sizeof...(rest) == 0`
UB at runtime: `rest` is uninitialized
Well-formed, but referring to `rest` is ill-formed
Compile error: not enough elements even without the pack

questions.prompt: Given `const auto& [a, ...rest] = t;`. What reference-ness do the elements of the pack `rest` have?
options: Independent copies (by value), as with `auto`
Non-const lvalue references, as with `auto&`
Const lvalue references into `t`
rvalue references regardless of the qualifier

questions.prompt: Which pack-indexing operations from `m1-l1` apply to a pack obtained from a structured binding?
options: None -- it's a special kind of pack with its own rules
Only `sizeof...`, but not `...[N]`
`...[N]`, `sizeof...`, and `...` expansion -- the same operations as for any other pack
Only `...` expansion, no indexing or `sizeof...`

lessons.title: #embed
lessons.motivation:
Embedding the contents of an arbitrary file (an image, a font, firmware, test data) directly into an executable is a routine task: the resource needs to be available to the program without reading from disk at runtime. Before C++26, the language had no built-in mechanism for this at all — you had to reach for external tools: run the file through `xxd -i` or similar to get a `.h` with a byte array and `#include` it, or embed the file as an object via `objcopy`/the linker and define extern symbols for the start and end of the section, or write a custom code-generation step in the build system. Every one of these paths drags in an external dependency, an extra build step, and format coordination between the generator and the code that uses it.

**`#embed`** (proposal P1967, originally adopted into C23 and then carried over into C++26) is a preprocessor directive that, during preprocessing, reads a file and substitutes its bytes as a comma-separated list of integer literals — ready to drop straight into an array initializer. No external tools, no separate build step: `#embed` sits alongside `#include`, but instead of inserting C++ text, it inserts data.
lessons.theory:
`#embed` is a preprocessor directive whose syntax mirrors `#include`: `#embed "file.bin"` searches next to the current file (like `#include "..."`), while `#embed <file.bin>` searches the include paths (like `#include <...>`). The directive's result is **neither a string nor an expression**, but a sequence of zero or more comma-separated **integer-constant tokens**: the file's bytes, each represented as an integer literal in the `unsigned char` range (`0`..`255`). This sequence is substituted into the source text exactly where the directive stood — just as `#include` substitutes a file's contents as text.

**Where this is legal to use.** Since the result is a comma-separated list of constant expressions, it fits anywhere a list of initializers is syntactically expected: inside an aggregate's braced-init-list (`const unsigned char data[] = { #embed "file.bin" };`), including `constexpr`/`static constexpr`. If `#embed` (taking `limit`, see below, into account) yields exactly one token, it can also serve as the sole initializer of a scalar — `int x = { #embed "f.bin" limit(1) };` is well-formed. But `int x = { #embed "f.bin" limit(2) };` is **ill-formed**: a scalar expects exactly one initializer, while the directive supplied two, comma-separated — the same diagnostic as for `int x = {1, 2};`, just with the numbers coming from a file. Using `#embed` where a single bare expression is expected (e.g. directly as the operand of `+`) doesn't make sense and doesn't work: the result is a comma-separated token list, not a single prvalue.

**Parameters.** After the file path (in either form), one or more parameters can be supplied in parentheses:

- **`limit(N)`** — restricts the result to the file's first `N` bytes. If the file is shorter than `N`, all of its bytes are used (no error).
- **`prefix(...)` / `suffix(...)`** — a token list inserted before/after the file's bytes (comma-separated if there's at least one byte). Handy for adding a null terminator (`suffix(, '\0')`), or for wrapping each data "block" with its own markers.
- **`if_empty(...)`** — a token list substituted *in place of* the content if the resource (after `limit` is applied) turns out to be empty — i.e. yields zero bytes. If the resource isn't empty, `if_empty` is not used at all — it doesn't get appended *in addition to* the data, it fully replaces it in the empty case.

**Empty resource — a separate edge case.** `#embed` for a zero-length file (or with `limit(0)`) without `if_empty` expands to an **empty token sequence** — literally nothing. `const unsigned char data[] = { #embed "empty.bin" };` becomes `const unsigned char data[] = { };` — an empty braced-init-list for the array. GCC accepts this as an extension (a zero-size array), but under `-pedantic-errors` (strict ISO mode) it's **ill-formed**: "zero-size array". For a scalar, an empty result doesn't make sense at all — `int x = { #embed "empty.bin" };` would become `int x = {};`, which for a scalar means zero-initialization (also not what "embed the file" usually means). `if_empty(...)` is exactly the tool for predictable behavior in this case: it guarantees a non-empty, meaningful result regardless of the resource's content.

**`__has_embed`** is a feature-test operator analogous to `__has_include`, but **only available inside `#if`/`#elif`** (i.e. in the evaluated expression of a preprocessor conditional directive) — trying to use it as an ordinary C++ expression (`bool b = __has_embed(...)`) is ill-formed, "used outside of preprocessing directive". Syntax: `__has_embed("file")`, `__has_embed(<file>)`, with the same parameters (`limit`, `prefix`, `suffix`, `if_empty`) as `#embed` itself. Unlike `__has_include`, which yields a boolean 0/1, `__has_embed` is **three-valued**: it expands to one of the macros `__STDC_EMBED_NOT_FOUND__` (0 — resource not found), `__STDC_EMBED_FOUND__` (1 — resource found and non-empty), or `__STDC_EMBED_EMPTY__` (2 — resource found, but empty given `limit`). In `#if`, all three values except `__STDC_EMBED_NOT_FOUND__` (0) are true — so `#if __has_embed("file")` answers "can `#embed` be used here and produce something at all (data or explicit emptiness)", while distinguishing "found non-empty" from "found but empty" requires an explicit comparison against `__STDC_EMBED_FOUND__` / `__STDC_EMBED_EMPTY__`.

**Type and time of evaluation.** Each byte is an integer-constant-expression in the `unsigned char` value range, so the whole list is ordinary constant expressions, suitable for `constexpr`/`consteval` contexts and static initialization; no file is read at program runtime — everything is expanded by the preprocessor at compile time. The "logical" element type of the array is chosen by the programmer through the declaration (`unsigned char`, `char`, `std::byte` via `static_cast` in `prefix`/`suffix`, etc.) — the directive itself doesn't impose a type, only the value range of its tokens.

**Nuance checklist.** *Unevaluated contexts* (`decltype`, `sizeof`, `noexcept`, `requires`) — not directly applicable: `#embed` is expanded by the preprocessor before C++ semantic analysis even begins, so the evaluated/unevaluated distinction doesn't apply to the directive itself — by the time the compiler sees the code, it's already ordinary integer literals. *SFINAE/overload resolution/concepts* — not applicable: this is a preprocessor directive, not an expression or a template declaration, so substitution failure doesn't apply to it. *Value category* — each resulting token is an integer literal, i.e. a prvalue, like any `42` in source code; the directive itself has no "value category" of its own, only the resulting literals do. *Old alternatives* — `xxd -i` or similar generators producing a `.h` with a byte array plus `#include`, `objcopy`/linker-based resources with extern symbols for section boundaries, build-system code generation; all of these require an external tool and a separate build step, whereas `#embed` is pure preprocessor, with no external dependency. The old approaches are still needed when the resource must end up in a separate section/segment with special linking attributes (alignment, a dedicated memory segment) — `#embed` only provides initializer data, not control over placement in the object file. *Ill-formed vs UB vs diagnostic* — every irregular case (`__has_embed` outside `#if`; an empty result without `if_empty` in strict mode; a token count mismatched with the number of elements for a scalar) is **ill-formed at compile time**, diagnosed by the preprocessor/compiler; `#embed` never produces runtime UB, since it does nothing at program runtime at all. *Interaction with the familiar* — the directive's result is just text consisting of constant expressions, substituted before template processing, so it works identically in ordinary code and inside a template body/`constexpr` function; `static constexpr unsigned char data[] = { #embed ... };` is ordinary static constexpr array initialization, with no new rules of its own.

examples.title: Basic #embed with limit
examples.explanation: `#embed __FILE__` embeds the current file's own bytes -- a convenient self-contained way to demonstrate `#embed` without a separate resource. `limit(8)` takes only the first 8 bytes of the source. The file's first line starts with `#include`, and `35 105 110 99 108 117 100 101` are the ASCII codes of `#`, `i`, `n`, `c`, `l`, `u`, `d`, `e`. In real code you'd point `#embed` at an actual resource (an image, binary data, etc.) instead of `__FILE__`.

examples.title: prefix/suffix: a null-terminated string from file bytes
examples.explanation: `suffix(, '\0')` appends one more token -- `'\0'` -- after the eight embedded bytes, with a leading comma as the initializer-list syntax requires. The result is a 9-element array: the 8 bytes of `#include` plus a null terminator, making it usable as a C string for printing. `prefix(...)` works symmetrically, adding tokens before the data.

examples.title: __has_embed: the three-valued feature test
examples.expectedOutput:
self: FOUND
self limit(0): EMPTY
missing: NOT_FOUND
examples.explanation: `__has_embed` is only available in `#if`/`#elif` and expands to one of three values: `__STDC_EMBED_FOUND__` (the resource exists and is non-empty -- true for the file itself with no `limit`), `__STDC_EMBED_EMPTY__` (the resource exists, but `limit(0)` would yield zero bytes), and `__STDC_EMBED_NOT_FOUND__` (the resource doesn't exist at all). All three branches here turn out true for their respective conditions, so all three lines print -- this is a demonstration, not typical code: in practice you'd usually check one specific resource right before `#embed`-ing it.

examples.title: if_empty: a predictable result for an empty resource
examples.explanation: `limit(0)` forces the directive to "see" zero bytes regardless of the file's actual size. Without `if_empty`, this would yield an empty `{ }` (on GCC without `-pedantic-errors`, a zero-size array extension; in strict ISO mode, ill-formed). `if_empty(0xDE, 0xAD)` is substituted *in place of* the empty result entirely, giving a two-byte array of known values: `0xDE = 222`, `0xAD = 173`.

exercises.explanation: The file starts with `#include`. `limit(4)` takes the first 4 bytes: `#`, `i`, `n`, `c` -- ASCII codes `35, 105, 110, 99`.

`limit(2)` yields two comma-separated integer tokens, but `x` is a scalar (`int`), which expects exactly one initializer. The error is on line 4 (`int x = {`): "scalar object 'x' requires one element in initializer" -- the same diagnostic as for `int x = {1, 2};`. For a scalar, use `limit(1)`.

options: In any C++ expression, e.g. `bool ok = __has_embed("f.bin");`
Only inside `#if`/`#elif` -- as the condition of a preprocessor directive
Only inside `#embed` itself, as one of its parameters
Only in `static_assert`
exercises.explanation: `__has_embed` is a preprocessor feature-test operator, like `__has_include`. Using it as an ordinary C++ expression is ill-formed ("used outside of preprocessing directive"); it's only legal in `#if`/`#elif` conditions.

exercises.prompt: `#embed "empty.bin"` for a zero-length file, with no `if_empty` parameter, is used as `const unsigned char data[] = { #embed "empty.bin" };`. What happens?
options: The directive substitutes a single byte with value 0
The directive expands to an empty token sequence -- yielding `data[] = { }`; in strict ISO mode (`-pedantic-errors`) this is ill-formed as a zero-size array
Preprocessor error: an empty file cannot be passed to `#embed`
Runtime UB on the first access to `data`
exercises.explanation: An empty resource (or `limit(0)`) without `if_empty` expands to nothing -- literally no tokens at all. For an array this gives an empty braced-init-list, which GCC accepts as an extension (a zero-size array), but strict ISO C++ considers ill-formed. `if_empty(...)` exists precisely to avoid this ambiguity and guarantee a non-empty result.

questions.prompt: What does the directive `#embed "file.bin"` expand to?
options: A string literal containing the file's contents
A comma-separated list of integer literals (the file's bytes)
A function call that reads the file at runtime
The file's path as a `const char*`

questions.prompt: What does the `limit(N)` parameter do?
options: Restricts the result to the file's first N bytes (or all bytes if the file is shorter than N)
Requires the file to be exactly N bytes, otherwise an error
Repeats the file's contents N times
Sets the resulting array's size in bytes, padding with zeros

questions.prompt: What is the `if_empty(...)` parameter for?
options: Appends tokens after the data, if the file is non-empty
Is substituted in place of the data only if the resource (after applying limit) yields zero bytes
Checks at compile time that the file is non-empty, and otherwise stops the build
Sets a default value for each byte if that byte equals zero

questions.prompt: When does the `#embed` directive read the file?
options: On the first access to the array at program runtime
During preprocessing, before the C++ code is compiled -- the result becomes constant expressions in the source text
At link time, like `objcopy`-based resources
Lazily, the first time the program runs on the target machine

lessons.title: Loose ends: variadic `friend` and `static_assert` messages
lessons.motivation:
This lesson bundles two small but independent C++26 improvements — both remove narrow but real friction in template code.

**Variadic `friend` (P2893).** Before C++26, `friend` could not be "expanded" over a parameter pack: `friend Ts...;` was a syntax error. If a class template needs to grant access to its private members to a set of types given by a pack (e.g. the passkey pattern, dispatching messages to a set of handlers, befriending a per-instantiation test class), you either had to write a `friend` for each type by hand (impossible for an arbitrary pack — the number of friends is unknown when the template is written), or work around the limitation through an intermediate "key" class that the types had to inherit from. Both options add a level of indirection purely to satisfy a syntactic restriction unrelated to the actual semantics of friendship.

**User-generated `static_assert` messages (P2741).** Before C++26, the second argument of `static_assert` had to be a string literal (optional since C++17, but a literal if present). That meant a template's error message could never include compile-time-computed values — `sizeof(T)`, a type's name, a numeric template parameter. A library could either write a generic message ("invalid type size") or resort to macros and preprocessor string concatenation to parametrize it somewhat — still without access to actual computed values. C++26 lets `static_assert` take any constant expression that yields an object with suitable `data()`/`size()` — for example the result of a `constexpr` call to `std::format`.
lessons.theory:
## Variadic `friend` (P2893 "Variadic Friends")

**Syntax.** C++26 adds a new form of friend declaration — the *friend-type-declaration*: after `friend`, one or more *friend-type-specifier*s are listed, separated by commas, each optionally followed by `...` to expand a pack:

```cpp
template <typename... Friends>
class Vault {
    friend Friends...;   // pack expansion: each type in Friends becomes a friend
};
```

This form is orthogonal to ordinary `friend` declarations for functions, and the two can be mixed: a single class can have both a regular `friend SomeClass;` and a `friend Pack...;`.

**Expansion semantics.** `friend Friends...;` for `Friends = {A, B, C}` is equivalent to three separate declarations `friend A; friend B; friend C;`. Each type in the pack that, at the point of instantiation, is a (possibly cv-qualified) class type is declared a friend — i.e. gets access to the enclosing class's private/protected members. The order of types in the pack does not matter, same as for ordinary `friend`.

**Empty pack.** If `Friends` is an empty pack (e.g. `Vault<>`), `friend Friends...;` expands to an empty list of declarations: no effect, no error. This is a natural consequence of the general rule for expanding empty packs in C++ — an empty pack yields an empty result.

**Non-class types in the pack.** If an element of the pack is not a class type (e.g. `int`, a pointer, a reference, an uninstantiated template, etc.), the corresponding friend declaration is **silently ignored** — this is not a compilation error. The wording deliberately mirrors the long-standing rule for ordinary `friend`: `friend int;` has always been a legal no-op, and `friend Ts...;` with `int` in the pack inherits that same behavior — each element of the pack is processed independently under the same rules as if it had been written as a single `friend`.

**Types only, not functions.** The variadic friend is a **friend-type-declaration**, so it applies only to friend *types* (classes and class templates). Friend functions (`friend void f();`, `friend T::method;`, etc.) are not expanded by this mechanism — C++26 did not add syntax for packs of friend functions; that restriction remains.

**A `friend` declaration does not introduce new names.** The general rule for `friend` still applies: `friend Friends...;` does not declare new entities and does not make the names in `Friends` visible in the class scope — it is purely about granting access to existing types, nothing more.

**The old alternative.** Before C++26, the problem was solved through an extra level of indirection — for example the "passkey" pattern: a separate "key" class is created with a `private` constructor, and every type that needs access is declared a friend of the *key* (one `friend` per type — the same problem, one level down, but at least localized). Alternatively: instead of befriending the pack directly, befriend a wrapper template parametrized by the same pack, and route access through that wrapper. Both paths add code and a level of indirection that `friend Friends...;` makes unnecessary for the most common case — "grant access to exactly the types in the pack".

**Interaction with templates and instantiation.** The friend list for `Vault<A, B>` and `Vault<A, C>` differs: the friend declaration depends on the template arguments of the specific specialization, like any other code dependent on template parameters. A type that is a friend of one specialization does not automatically become a friend of another specialization of the same template.

**Unevaluated contexts, value category, SFINAE.** A friend declaration is part of a class definition, not an expression; it does not produce a value and does not participate directly in `decltype`/`sizeof`/`noexcept`/`requires`. Friendship itself does not make access to private members part of a signature for SFINAE purposes: attempting to call a non-friend function that accesses a private member is a hard error at the point of use (inside the friend function's body, on instantiation), not a substitution failure — exactly as for ordinary `friend` before C++26. Nothing new here.

## User-generated `static_assert` messages (P2741 "User-generated static_assert messages")

**Syntax.** `static_assert(constant-expression, message)`, where `message` can now be **any constant expression**, not just a string literal. For such a `message` to be valid, the object `M` it denotes must satisfy two conditions:

- `M.data()` is implicitly convertible to `const char*` as a constant expression;
- `M.size()` is implicitly convertible to `std::size_t` as a constant expression.

So any type with `constexpr data()`/`size()` of the right "shape" qualifies — most notably `std::string_view`, `std::string` (in C++26 many `std::string` operations are `constexpr`), `char` arrays via a small wrapper, and in particular the result of a `constexpr` call to `std::format` (whose return type is `std::string`).

**Encoding.** Characters in the message must use the **ordinary literal encoding** — that is, not `u8"..."` (a UTF-8 string literal) and not other prefixed encodings. `static_assert(cond, u8"text")` is ill-formed: the compiler rejects the wide/u8 string at this position during parsing, regardless of the value of `cond`. An ordinary ("narrow") string literal `"text"` remains valid, as before — it is simply a special case of the new rule, since a string literal trivially has `data()`/`size()` of the required shape.

**When the message is evaluated.** `message` is a constant expression, so it is evaluated at compile time regardless of whether the check on `constant-expression` (the first argument) fails. If the first argument is true, the assertion passes and the message text **has no effect on the program** (but it must still be a valid constant expression — otherwise the program is ill-formed even though the assertion itself "would have passed"). If the first argument is false, the message text becomes part of the compiler's diagnostic: for example, `static_assert(sizeof(T) == 4, std::format("expected 4-byte type, got {}-byte type", sizeof(T)))` for `T = std::int64_t` produces `static assertion failed: expected 4-byte type, got 8-byte type` — the `8` is substituted from the actual `sizeof(T)` computed at instantiation.

**The old alternative.** Before C++26, since the message could only be a literal, parametrized diagnostics were done either via macros with preprocessor string concatenation (no access to computed values — only to what preprocessor substitution can express textually), or not done at all — leaving a generic message like "size mismatch" without specifics, forcing the programmer to dig out the actual type names/values via `__PRETTY_FUNCTION__`, comments, or external documentation.

**Ill-formed vs UB vs diagnostic.** An invalid `message` (e.g. `u8"..."`, or a type without suitable `data()`/`size()`, or a non-constexpr-evaluable expression) makes the program **ill-formed** at compile time — a compilation error, not UB and not a warning. The behavior of `static_assert` itself (a compile error when the condition is false) has not changed — what changed is what is allowed as the second argument and what the compiler can include in the diagnostic text.

**Interaction with templates and constexpr.** The main practical effect is that template error diagnostics become informative without extra effort: the message can include `sizeof(T)`, the name of the instantiated type (via `constexpr` serialization), values of non-type template parameters — everything that previously was visible only from the "required from here" instantiation chain, not from the text of the `static_assert` itself. Since `message` is a constant expression, it follows the same rules as any other constexpr computation in a template context: it must be evaluable for the specific specialization, otherwise the error occurs earlier (while evaluating the message itself), regardless of the value of the first argument.

**Unevaluated contexts, value category, SFINAE.** `static_assert` is a declaration, not an expression usable in `decltype`/`sizeof`/`noexcept` — these checklist items do not apply directly. As for SFINAE: a failing `static_assert` inside a function/template body at instantiation time is a **hard error**, not a substitution failure, exactly as before C++26; the new message makes that hard error more informative but does not turn it into an SFINAE-friendly check (conditionally excluding overloads still requires `requires`/`enable_if`/concepts, not `static_assert`).

examples.title: Variadic friend: access for a pack of types
examples.explanation: `friend Friends...;` in `Account<Auditor, Teller>` expands to `friend Auditor; friend Teller;` — both types get access to the private `balance_`. `Teller::deposit` increases the balance by 50, `Auditor::balance` reads the result: `100 + 50 = 150`. Before C++26, doing this for an arbitrary pack of friends would require an intermediate "key" class — befriending a pack directly was a syntax error.

examples.title: Empty pack and non-class types in the pack
examples.explanation: Both classes compile cleanly despite `Extra...` being empty for `Box<>` and containing fundamental types for `Box<int, double>`. An empty pack expands to an empty list of friend declarations — no effect. `int` and `double` are not class types, so the corresponding friend declarations are silently ignored (the same behavior as a standalone `friend int;` before C++26). The ordinary `friend Logger;` declared alongside the pack keeps working as before and grants access to the private `peek()` in both cases.

exercises.explanation: `friend Friends...;` for `Counter<Incrementer, Reader>` makes both `Incrementer` and `Reader` friends — both get access to the private `value_`. Two calls to `Incrementer::bump(c)` raise `value_` from 10 to 12, and `Reader::get(c)` returns the final value.

Line 5 reads `friend Friends;` — without `...`. `Friends` is a parameter pack here, and a friend declaration over a pack must be written as `friend Friends...;`. GCC rejects this with "parameter packs not expanded with '...'": compilation fails before `Sensor<Calibrator>` is even instantiated, regardless of `Calibrator` itself correctly accessing `reading_`.

A `static_assert` message must use the ordinary literal encoding, but `u8"type must be 4 bytes"` is a UTF-8 string literal. C++26 allows an arbitrary constant expression as the message, but not encoding-prefixed strings (`u8`, `u`, `U`, `L`): line 5 fails to compile with "a wide string is invalid in this context", regardless of `sizeof(int) == 4` (the condition itself would have passed).

options: The program compiles: a `static_assert` with a computed message is only checked when the function is called, not when the template is instantiated
Compilation fails; the diagnostic includes the text "static assertion failed: expected 4-byte type, got 8-byte type" — with the actual sizeof(T) substituted in
Compilation fails, but the message text is ignored by the compiler — only the generic text "static assertion failed" is shown
Compilation fails while evaluating the message itself (`std::format` cannot be called inside static_assert), regardless of the value of sizeof(T)
exercises.explanation: For `T = std::int64_t` (`sizeof(T) == 8`), the condition `sizeof(T) == 4` is false, so `static_assert` fires. The message is a `constexpr` call to `std::format` satisfying the `data()`/`size()` requirements, so the compiler evaluates it and includes it in the diagnostic. Verified on GCC trunk: `error: static assertion failed: expected 4-byte type, got 8-byte type` — the 8 comes from the computed `sizeof(T)`.

questions.prompt: What does `friend Friends...;` do inside `template <typename... Friends> class Vault { ... };`?
options: Declares a new member function `Friends` with a variable number of arguments
Expands into one `friend` declaration per type in the pack `Friends`
Makes only the first type in the pack a friend
It is a syntax error — `friend` cannot be applied to parameter packs

questions.prompt: The pack `Friends` used in `friend Friends...;` contains a non-class type (e.g. `int`). What happens?
options: Compilation error: a friend declaration only allows class types
The corresponding friend declaration is silently ignored (a no-op), same as a standalone `friend int;`
The program compiles, and `int` becomes a friend — with no practical effect besides a warning
The behavior is unspecified and compiler-dependent

questions.prompt: Before C++26, how could you approximate `friend Friends...;` for an arbitrary pack of types?
options: You couldn't — the problem was fundamentally unsolvable before C++26
Use `friend` with a wrapper template or an intermediate "key" class that the types needing access inherit from — an extra level of indirection
Declare all possible friends as `public` members of the class
Use `static_assert` instead of `friend`

questions.prompt: In C++26, the second argument to `static_assert` is a `constexpr` call returning `std::string`. What does the standard require of this object?
options: It must be `std::string_view`, and only that
`data()` must convert to `const char*` and `size()` to `std::size_t`, both as constant expressions; the character encoding must be ordinary (not `u8`/`u`/`L`)
The object must be a string literal expanded through a `constexpr` function
It only needs to be `constexpr`-constructible — there are no requirements on `data()`/`size()`
